question.text: Scenario: Mr. Vikram Choudhary, aged 45, is an ultra-HNI with a total investment portfolio of Rs. 25 crore. His current allocation is: Equity Mutual Funds Rs. 8 crore, Fixed Deposits Rs. 5 crore, Direct Equity Rs. 5 crore, Real Estate Rs. 4 crore, Gold Rs. 1.5 crore, and Cash Rs. 1.5 crore. His annual income is Rs. 3 crore. His investment adviser, Ms. Neha Sharma, suggests allocating Rs. 3 crore (12% of portfolio) to Alternative Investment Funds for diversification. She proposes: Option 1 — Category I AIF (Venture Capital Fund): Invests in early-stage tech startups. Minimum Rs. 1 crore. Close-ended with 7-year lock-in. Target return 20-25% IRR. Manager continuing interest 2.5% of corpus. Fund corpus Rs. 200 crore with maximum 250 investors. Option 2 — Category II AIF (Private Equity Fund): Invests in mid-market growth companies. Minimum Rs. 1 crore. Close-ended with 5-year lock-in plus 2-year extension option. Target return 15-18% IRR. No leverage permitted except for day-to-day operations. Management fee 2% + carried interest 20% above 8% hurdle. Option 3 — Category III AIF (Long-Short Equity Hedge Fund): Employs long-short equity strategies using derivatives. Minimum Rs. 1 crore. Open-ended with 1-year lock-in. Target return 12-15% with low correlation to equity markets. Manager continuing interest 5% of corpus. Can employ leverage through derivatives. Vikram qualifies as an accredited investor (annual income ≥ Rs. 2 crore). Question 5 of 5: As an accredited investor, Vikram can potentially invest in some AIFs with a reduced minimum ticket size of Rs. 25 lakh instead of Rs. 1 crore. Which statement about this facility is correct?

answer.text: All AIFs must offer reduced minimums to accredited investors
Accredited investor facility is optional — not all AIFs adopt it, and reduced ticket size does not reduce investment risk
Accredited investors get guaranteed returns from AIFs
SEBI mandates all AIFs to accept accredited investors at Rs. 25 lakh minimum

explanation.text: **The Logic:** The accredited investor concept is optional — it is not compulsory for AIFs to adopt it. Only a few AIFs have chosen to offer reduced minimums of Rs. 25 lakh to accredited investors. The reduced ticket size does NOT reduce the inherent investment risk of the AIF strategy. **The Trap:** Reduced minimum doesn't mean reduced risk — an investor putting Rs. 25 lakh in a hedge fund faces the same strategy risk as one putting Rs. 1 crore. **Cross-Exam Context:** Accredited investor provisions are tested in X-A for understanding AIF access frameworks. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Even at Rs. 25 lakh, AIF investments remain high-risk — the accredited investor framework is about demonstrating financial sophistication, not about making AIFs safer. Only use this for clients who truly understand and can absorb AIF-level risk.
